[Systems]
Lecture for L3 – SE2 (2025/2026) Prof.Babahenini Mohamed Chaouki Mohamed Khider Biskra University Computer Science Department September 23, 2025 Pr.Babahenini Med Chaouki (Biskra Univ.) Process Management in OS September 23, 2025 1 / 30
title: Systems
subject: se

text:
[Outline]
1 Introduction to Processes 2 Process Control Block 3 Process Lifecycle 4 Resources and Management 5 Process Scheduling 6 Resource Management vs. Process Management 7 Context Switching 8 Challenges and Modern Issues Pr.Babahenini Med Chaouki (Biskra Univ.) Process Management in OS September 23, 2025 2 / 30 Introduction to Processes
title: Outline
subject: se

text:
[Process?]
Definition A process is a program in execution, including the program code, current activity, and allocated resources. Program = passive entity (instructions on disk). Process = active execution (instance running in memory). Example: A text editor file ( gedit.exe ) vs. an open editing session. Pr.Babahenini Med Chaouki (Biskra Univ.) Process Management in OS September 23, 2025 3 / 30 Process Control Block
title: Process?
subject: se

text:
[Definition]
Definition The Process Control Block (PCB) is a data structure maintained by the operating system to store all information needed to manage a process. Each active process has exactly one PCB. PCBs are stored in the process table of the OS. Used by the scheduler to decide which process to run. Pr.Babahenini Med Chaouki (Biskra Univ.) Process Management in OS September 23, 2025 4 / 30 Process Control Block
title: Definition
subject: se

text:
[PCB]
Process Identification: Process ID (PID), Parent PID, User ID. Processor State: Program Counter, CPU registers, Flags. Memory Management Info: Base/limit registers, page tables, segment tables. I/O and File Management: List of open files, I/O devices allocated, sockets. Scheduling Information: Priority, Scheduling queue pointers, CPU usage statistics. Accounting Information: User/system CPU time consumed, job or process number. Pr.Babahenini Med Chaouki (Biskra Univ.) Process Management in OS September 23, 2025 5 / 30 Process Control Block
title: PCB
subject: se

text:
[Illustration]
Process Identification: PID, PPID, UID Processor State: PC, CPU Registers, Flags Memory Info: Base/Limit Registers, Page Tables I/O and File Info: Open Files, Devices, Sockets Scheduling Info: Priority, Queue Pointers, CPU Usage Accounting Info: Time Used, Process Number Simplified structure of a PCB Pr.Babahenini Med Chaouki (Biskra Univ.) Process Management in OS September 23, 2025 6 / 30 Process Control Block
title: Illustration
subject: se

text:
[PCB]
The PCB is essential for context switching . Without PCB, the OS could not resume processes correctly. Example (Linux): Linux stores PCB in the task struct kernel structure. Contains over 300 fields: state, PID, scheduling info, memory maps, file descriptors. The efficiency of PCB handling directly affects OS performance . Pr.Babahenini Med Chaouki (Biskra Univ.) Process Management in OS September 23, 2025 7 / 30 Process Lifecycle
title: PCB
subject: se

text:
[States]
New: Process is being created. Ready: Process is in memory, waiting to be assigned CPU. Running: CPU is executing process instructions. Waiting (Blocked): Process is waiting for an event (e.g., I/O completion). Terminated: Process has finished execution and is removed from memory. Remark: This is the five-state model , used in most textbooks and operating systems. Pr.Babahenini Med Chaouki (Biskra Univ.) Process Management in OS September 23, 2025 8 / 30 Process Lifecycle
title: States
subject: se

text:
[Model)]
New Ready Running Waiting Terminated Admitted Dispatch Interrupt I/O Request I/O Done Exit Pr.Babahenini Med Chaouki (Biskra Univ.) Process Management in OS September 23, 2025 9 / 30 Process Lifecycle
title: Model)
subject: se

text:
[OS)]
Some operating systems introduce more states: Suspended Ready: Process is ready but swapped out of main memory (in secondary storage). Suspended Waiting: Process is blocked and swapped out. Zombie: Terminated but still has an entry in process table (waiting for parent to collect exit status). These states improve OS flexibility in memory-constrained or multi-user systems. Pr.Babahenini Med Chaouki (Biskra Univ.) Process Management in OS September 23, 2025 10 / 30 Process Lifecycle
title: OS)
subject: se

text:
[States]
Pr.Babahenini Med Chaouki (Biskra Univ.) Process Management in OS September 23, 2025 11 / 30 Process Lifecycle
title: States
subject: se

text:
[Example]
New → Ready: User launches a browser, process is created and loaded into memory. Ready → Running: Scheduler gives CPU to the browser. Running → Waiting: Browser waits for data from the network. Waiting → Ready: Network data arrives, browser returns to ready queue. Running → Terminated: User closes the browser, process ends. Conclusion: State transitions reflect dynamic interactions with CPU, memory, and I/O. Pr.Babahenini Med Chaouki (Biskra Univ.) Process Management in OS September 23, 2025 12 / 30 Process Lifecycle
title: Example
subject: se

text:
[Models]
Classical (5 states): New Ready Running Waiting Terminated Modern Extensions: Suspended Ready Suspended Waiting Zombie Orphan (child without parent) Remark: Modern OS add states to handle memory swapping and process cleanup. Pr.Babahenini Med Chaouki (Biskra Univ.) Process Management in OS September 23, 2025 13 / 30 Resources and Management
title: Models
subject: se

text:
[Resource?]
Definition A resource is any physical or virtual component of a computer system that a process needs to execute. Resources are limited and must be shared among processes. Without proper allocation, processes may block each other (deadlock). Examples: CPU cycles (processor time). Memory (RAM, cache, swap space). I/O Devices (printers, disks, network cards). Files and databases. Modern resources: GPUs, network bandwidth, cloud VMs. Pr.Babahenini Med Chaouki (Biskra Univ.) Process Management in OS September 23, 2025 14 / 30 Resources and Management
title: Resource?
subject: se

text:
[Classification]
By Nature: Hardware resources: CPU, memory, disk, I/O devices. Software resources: files, semaphores, database locks. By Availability: Preemptible resources: can be taken back from a process (CPU, memory). Non-preemptible resources: must be released voluntarily (printer, file lock). By Duration of Use: Reusable: can be released and used by others (memory, CPU). Consumable: used up when acquired (messages, signals, network packets). Pr.Babahenini Med Chaouki (Biskra Univ.) Process Management in OS September 23, 2025 15 / 30 Resources and Management
title: Classification
subject: se

text:
[Diagram]
Resources By Nature Hardware Software By Availability PreemptibleNon-preemptible By Duration Reusable Consumable Pr.Babahenini Med Chaouki (Biskra Univ.) Process Management in OS September 23, 2025 16 / 30 Resources and Management
title: Diagram
subject: se

text:
[OS]
Linux: Files are treated as resources, accessed through file descriptors. Devices (e.g., printers, disks) appear as special files in /dev . Memory managed via paging (frames as resources). Windows: Uses handles for resource management (files, processes, events). Registry keys act as managed software resources. Cloud/Distributed Systems: Virtual Machines (VMs), containers, and network bandwidth treated as resources. Pr.Babahenini Med Chaouki (Biskra Univ.) Process Management in OS September 23, 2025 17 / 30 Resources and Management
title: OS
subject: se

text:
[Graph]
A Resource Allocation Graph (RAG) is used to model the allocation and request of resources in the system. Components of the graph: Nodes: Represent processes or resources. Edges: Request edge: Process requests a resource. Assignment edge: Resource is allocated to a process. Used to detect and prevent deadlocks. Pr.Babahenini Med Chaouki (Biskra Univ.) Process Management in OS September 23, 2025 18 / 30 Resources and Management
title: Graph
subject: se

text:
[Example]
P1 P2 R1 R2 Request Assignment Request Assignment Pr.Babahenini Med Chaouki (Biskra Univ.) Process Management in OS September 23, 2025 19 / 30 Process Scheduling
title: Example
subject: se

text:
[Scheduling]
The CPU scheduler is responsible for selecting which process should be executed next. Scheduling Criteria: CPU Utilization: keep CPU busy. Throughput: maximize completed processes. Turnaround Time: minimize job completion delay. Waiting Time: reduce time in ready queue. Response Time: important in interactive systems. Pr.Babahenini Med Chaouki (Biskra Univ.) Process Management in OS September 23, 2025 20 / 30 Process Scheduling
title: Scheduling
subject: se

text:
[Algorithms]
First-Come, First-Served (FCFS) Shortest Job First (SJF) Priority Scheduling Round Robin (RR) Multilevel Queue Scheduling Pr.Babahenini Med Chaouki (Biskra Univ.) Process Management in OS September 23, 2025 21 / 30 Process Scheduling
title: Algorithms
subject: se

text:
[2)]
3 processes with arrival at t = 0 : P 1 : burst time = 5 units P 2 : burst time = 3 units P 3 : burst time = 7 units Quantum = 2 units Time P1 P2 P3 P1 P3 P1 P3 0 2 4 6 8 10 11 13 Round Robin execution order with quantum = 2 Pr.Babahenini Med Chaouki (Biskra Univ.) Process Management in OS September 23, 2025 22 / 30 Resource Management vs. Process Management
subject: se

text:
[Management]
Processes are entities that require resources to complete their tasks. Process management: Manages process execution (scheduling, creation, termination, etc.). Resource management: Manages the allocation and control of system resources (CPU, memory, I/O devices). Effective resource allocation ensures that processes have the resources needed to run efficiently without waste or conflict. Pr.Babahenini Med Chaouki (Biskra Univ.) Process Management in OS September 23, 2025 23 / 30 Resource Management vs. Process Management
title: Management
subject: se

text:
[Management]
Deadlocks: Occur when a set of processes are blocked because each process is holding a resource and waiting for another resource. Starvation: A process may wait indefinitely if resources are never allocated to it. Fairness: The OS must ensure that all processes get a fair share of resources. Concurrency: Managing multiple processes that request resources simultaneously. Pr.Babahenini Med Chaouki (Biskra Univ.) Process Management in OS September 23, 2025 24 / 30 Context Switching
title: Management
subject: se

text:
[Switching?]
Context Switching: The process of saving the state of a currently running process and loading the state of another process. This is required to allow multiple processes to share a CPU (multiprogramming). Context switches are expensive in terms of performance because they involve overhead. Steps of context switching : Switching CPU from one process to another. Save: program counter, registers, memory maps. Restore: state of next scheduled process. Overhead: CPU does no useful work during switch. Optimizations: hardware support, lightweight threads. Pr.Babahenini Med Chaouki (Biskra Univ.) Process Management in OS September 23, 2025 25 / 30 Challenges and Modern Issues
title: Switching?
subject: se

text:
[Management]
Deadlocks: Two or more processes wait forever for resources held by each other. Starvation: A process waits indefinitely because higher-priority processes monopolize resources. Fairness: The OS must ensure equitable CPU and resource distribution. Concurrency: Handling simultaneous execution of multiple processes correctly. Overhead: Context switching and synchronization consume CPU time. Pr.Babahenini Med Chaouki (Biskra Univ.) Process Management in OS September 23, 2025 26 / 30 Challenges and Modern Issues
title: Management
subject: se

text:
[Example]
Scenario Process P1 holds Resource R1 and requests R2. Process P2 holds Resource R2 and requests R1. Both are waiting forever → deadlock. P1 P2 R1 R2 holds requests holds requests Circular wait deadlock between P1, P2, R1, R2 Pr.Babahenini Med Chaouki (Biskra Univ.) Process Management in OS September 23, 2025 27 / 30 Challenges and Modern Issues
title: Example
subject: se

text:
[Fairness]
Starvation: A process never gets CPU or I/O time. Example: In priority scheduling, low-priority jobs may never execute. Fairness: OS must prevent monopolization of resources. Example: Round Robin ensures time fairness in time-sharing systems. Solutions: Aging: gradually increase priority of waiting processes. Fair-share scheduling: divide CPU proportionally among users. Pr.Babahenini Med Chaouki (Biskra Univ.) Process Management in OS September 23, 2025 28 / 30 Challenges and Modern Issues
title: Fairness
subject: se

text:
[Synchronization]
Multiple processes/threads may access shared data. Problems: Race conditions (inconsistent results). Critical section conflicts. Solutions: Locks, semaphores, monitors. Transactional memory in modern CPUs. Example: Two bank transactions updating the same account balance. Pr.Babahenini Med Chaouki (Biskra Univ.) Process Management in OS September 23, 2025 29 / 30 Challenges and Modern Issues
title: Synchronization
subject: se

text:
[Overhead]
Context Switching: Saving/restoring PCBs consumes CPU cycles. Scheduling: Complex policies add decision overhead. Synchronization: Locks and semaphores can reduce throughput. Trade-off: Balance between fairness, responsiveness, and performance. Pr.Babahenini Med Chaouki (Biskra Univ.) Process Management in OS September 23, 2025 30 / 30
title: Overhead
subject: se

text:
[Processes]
Lecture for L3 – SE2 (2025/2026) Prof.Babahenini Mohamed Chaouki Mohamed Khider Biskra University Computer Science Department October 2, 2025 Pr.Babahenini Med Chaouki (Biskra Univ.) ME October 2, 2025 1 / 25
title: Processes
subject: se

text:
[Outline]
1 Introduction to Mutual Exclusion 2 The Critical Section Problem 3 Solutions to Mutual Exclusion 4 Hardware Solutions to Mutual Exclusion 5 Semaphores and Mutexes 6 Other Mutual Exclusion Mechanisms Pr.Babahenini Med Chaouki (Biskra Univ.) ME October 2, 2025 2 / 25 Introduction to Mutual Exclusion
title: Outline
subject: se

text:
[Exclusion?]
Mutual Exclusion: is a fundamental concept in concurrent programming and operating systems that ensures only one process (or thread) can access a shared resource (like memory, files, or critical sections of code) at a time. Prevents race conditions where processes might access shared resources (e.g., variables, files) simultaneously, leading to unpredictable results. Pr.Babahenini Med Chaouki (Biskra Univ.) ME October 2, 2025 3 / 25 Introduction to Mutual Exclusion
title: Exclusion?
subject: se

text:
[Exclusion?]
Mutual Exclusion is a property of process synchronization that states that “no two processes can exist in the critical section at any given point of time“. Used in concurrent programming to avoid the simultaneous use of a common resource, such as a global variable, by pieces of computer code called critical sections. There are several kinds of concurrent execution: Interrupt handlers. Interleaved, preemptively scheduled processes/threads Multiprocessor clusters, with shared memory Distributed systems Pr.Babahenini Med Chaouki (Biskra Univ.) ME October 2, 2025 4 / 25 Introduction to Mutual Exclusion
title: Exclusion?
subject: se

text:
[Examples]
1 ATM Machine Example: Two people want to withdraw money from the same bank account using two different ATMs at the same time. If both ATMs access the account simultaneously without coordination, one might withdraw before the other’s balance update is processed → leading to an inconsistent balance. To prevent this, the bank enforces mutual exclusion: only one ATM transaction on that account can be processed at a time. 2 Traffic Intersection Example: At a crossroads with no traffic lights: If two cars from different directions try to cross at the same time, there’s a collision (like a race condition). A traffic light or stop sign ensures only one direction moves at a time → this is mutual exclusion. 3 Bathroom Lock Example: In a shared office: A bathroom has a lock on the door. When one person enters and locks the door, others must wait outside. This ensures only one person uses it at a time → the lock is like a mutex (mutual exclusion lock). Pr.Babahenini Med Chaouki (Biskra Univ.) ME October 2, 2025 5 / 25 The Critical Section Problem
title: Examples
subject: se

text:
[Section?]
A Critical Section is the sensitive part of a program where shared resources are accessed. Since multiple processes may want to access these resources at the same time, the critical section is the place where race conditions can occur. It must be protected to ensure only one process enters at a time. The goal is to design a protocol that ensures: No two processes can be in the critical section at the same time (mutual exclusion). Progress is guaranteed (no process waits indefinitely). Bounded waiting (a process will enter the critical section within a bounded time). Pr.Babahenini Med Chaouki (Biskra Univ.) ME October 2, 2025 6 / 25 The Critical Section Problem
title: Section?
subject: se

text:
[Solution]
Any solution to the critical section problem must satisfy: Mutual Exclusion: No two processes can be in their critical section simultaneously. Progress: If no process is in the critical section and some processes want to enter, the selection of the process that will enter must be made without indefinite delay. Bounded Waiting: There must be a limit on how many times other processes can enter their critical sections before a waiting process gets its chance. Fairness: All processes should have equal opportunity to access shared resources or critical sections. No process should be favored or given special privileges that allow it to unfairly access or manipulate shared resources. Pr.Babahenini Med Chaouki (Biskra Univ.) ME October 2, 2025 7 / 25 The Critical Section Problem
title: Solution
subject: se

text:
[Environment]
A Critical Section Environment contains: Entry Section Code requesting entry into the critical section. Critical Section Code in which only one process can execute at any one time. Exit Section The end of the critical section, releasing or allowing others in. Remainder Section Rest of the code AFTER the critical section. Pr.Babahenini Med Chaouki (Biskra Univ.) ME October 2, 2025 8 / 25 The Critical Section Problem
title: Environment
subject: se

text:
[Transactions]
Imagine a scenario where multiple processes represent transactions on a bank account. Each process may perform operations like depositing or withdrawing money from the account. To ensure data consistency and prevent race conditions, we need to enforce mutual exclusion when accessing the account balance. Pr.Babahenini Med Chaouki (Biskra Univ.) ME October 2, 2025 9 / 25 The Critical Section Problem
title: Transactions
subject: se

text:
[Transactions]
Here’s how we can structure the example: Shared Resource: The shared resource is the bank account balance. Critical Section: The critical section is where the balance of the bank account is accessed or modified. Mutual Exclusion Mechanism: We will use a mutex (lock) to ensure that only one thread can access the bank account balance at any given time. Pr.Babahenini Med Chaouki (Biskra Univ.) ME October 2, 2025 10 / 25 Solutions to Mutual Exclusion
title: Transactions
subject: se

text:
[Variables]
Lock variables provide a simple mechanism for mutual exclusion. A lock is a variable used to indicate whether the critical section is in use: 0 (unlocked): The critical section is free. 1 (locked): A process is in the critical section. Example: while (lock != 0); lock = 1; // critical section lock = 0; Pr.Babahenini Med Chaouki (Biskra Univ.) ME October 2, 2025 11 / 25 Solutions to Mutual Exclusion
title: Variables
subject: se

text:
[Locks]
Busy waiting: Processes repeatedly check the lock, wasting CPU cycles. No guarantee of bounded waiting , as one process may repeatedly prevent others from entering the critical section. Inefficient for multi-core systems. Pr.Babahenini Med Chaouki (Biskra Univ.) ME October 2, 2025 12 / 25 Solutions to Mutual Exclusion
title: Locks
subject: se

text:
[(Overview)]
Purpose: First correct software solution to the Critical Section Problem . Works for two processes only. Uses two shared variables: flag[2] : intention to enter Critical Section. turn : decides which process gets priority. Satisfies: Mutual Exclusion Progress Bounded Waiting Pr.Babahenini Med Chaouki (Biskra Univ.) ME October 2, 2025 13 / 25 Solutions to Mutual Exclusion
title: (Overview)
subject: se

text:
[i)]
Algorithm while (true) { flag[i] = true; while (flag[j]) { if (turn == j) { flag[i] = false; while (turn == j); flag[i] = true; } } // Critical Section turn = j; flag[i] = false; // Remainder Section } Pr.Babahenini Med Chaouki (Biskra Univ.) ME October 2, 2025 14 / 25 Solutions to Mutual Exclusion
title: i)
subject: se

text:
[(Overview)]
Purpose: Classic software solution for the Critical Section Problem . Works for two processes only. Shared Variables: flag[2] : indicates intention to enter CS. turn : decides which process gets priority. Properties: Mutual Exclusion Progress Bounded Waiting Pr.Babahenini Med Chaouki (Biskra Univ.) ME October 2, 2025 15 / 25 Solutions to Mutual Exclusion
title: (Overview)
subject: se

text:
[i)]
Algorithm while (true) { flag[i] = true; // I want to enter turn = j; // Give priority to other process while (flag[j] && turn == j); // Busy wait // Critical Section ---- shared resource code ---- flag[i] = false; // Exit CS // Remainder Section } Pr.Babahenini Med Chaouki (Biskra Univ.) ME October 2, 2025 16 / 25 Solutions to Mutual Exclusion
title: i)
subject: se

text:
[Algorithm]
Feature Dekker’s Algorithm Peterson’s Algorithm Year / History Oldest known software solu- tion (1960s). Later, simpler solution (1981). Mechanism Uses flag[2] + turn , with complex back-off logic. Uses flag[2] + turn , very straightforward. Complexity More complicated with nested conditions. Much simpler and elegant. Fairness Ensures fairness (bounded waiting). Ensures fairness naturally via turn-taking. Efficiency Less efficient due to retries and back-off. More efficient, only one waiting condition. Usefulness Today Mainly of histori- cal/theoretical value. Still widely taught as the classic example. Pr.Babahenini Med Chaouki (Biskra Univ.) ME October 2, 2025 17 / 25 Hardware Solutions to Mutual Exclusion
title: Algorithm
subject: se

text:
[Systems)]
Idea: On a single-CPU system, only one process executes at a time. Context switching happens through interrupts . By disabling interrupts before entering the Critical Section , no process switch can occur. Re-enable interrupts after leaving the critical section. Pros: Simple to implement Guarantees mutual exclusion Cons: Works only on single-processor systems Delays or loses important interrupts Forgetting to re-enable → system crash Insecure: user processes shouldn’t disable interrupts Pr.Babahenini Med Chaouki (Biskra Univ.) ME October 2, 2025 18 / 25 Hardware Solutions to Mutual Exclusion
title: Systems)
subject: se

text:
[(Overview)]
TSL = Test-and-Set Lock A hardware instruction that reads a memory location and sets it to 1 atomically . Used to implement spinlocks for mutual exclusion. Works in both single and multiprocessor systems. Mechanism: Each process uses a shared lock variable. TSL checks and sets the lock in one indivisible step. If lock was free ( 0 ), process enters the Critical Section. If lock was taken ( 1 ), process keeps waiting (busy waiting). Pr.Babahenini Med Chaouki (Biskra Univ.) ME October 2, 2025 19 / 25 Hardware Solutions to Mutual Exclusion
title: (Overview)
subject: se

text:
[(Pseudo-code)]
Pseudo-code for Process i while (true) { do { TSL REGISTER, LOCK // Load lock into REGISTER and set LOCK = 1 } while (REGISTER != 0); // Repeat if lock was already set // Critical Section ---- shared resource code ---- LOCK = 0; // Release lock // Remainder Section } Pr.Babahenini Med Chaouki (Biskra Univ.) ME October 2, 2025 20 / 25 Hardware Solutions to Mutual Exclusion
title: (Pseudo-code)
subject: se

text:
[TSL]
Pros: Simple and effective. Works on multiprocessor systems. Cons: Causes busy waiting (wastes CPU cycles). Not fair — processes may starve. Can lead to priority inversion : lower-priority processes may hold the lock and block higher-priority ones. Not scalable for systems with many processes. Pr.Babahenini Med Chaouki (Biskra Univ.) ME October 2, 2025 21 / 25 Semaphores and Mutexes
title: TSL
subject: se

text:
[Semaphores]
A semaphore is a synchronization primitive introduced by Dijkstra. Two atomic operations: wait() and signal() . wait(S) : If S > 0 , decrement S ; otherwise, block the process. signal(S) : Increment S ; wake up a waiting process (if any). Useful for mutual exclusion and controlling resource access. Pr.Babahenini Med Chaouki (Biskra Univ.) ME October 2, 2025 22 / 25 Semaphores and Mutexes
title: Semaphores
subject: se

text:
[(Mutexes)]
Binary semaphores (or mutexes ) are a special case of semaphores where the value can only be 0 or 1. wait() locks the critical section, and signal() releases it. Ensures that only one process can enter the critical section at a time. Pr.Babahenini Med Chaouki (Biskra Univ.) ME October 2, 2025 23 / 25 Semaphores and Mutexes
title: (Mutexes)
subject: se

text:
[Example]
semaphore mutex = 1; wait(mutex); // critical section signal(mutex); If another process calls wait(mutex) while the first process is in the critical section, it will block until the semaphore is signaled. Pr.Babahenini Med Chaouki (Biskra Univ.) ME October 2, 2025 24 / 25 Other Mutual Exclusion Mechanisms
title: Example
subject: se

text:
[Monitors]
Monitors are high-level abstractions for managing mutual exclusion and synchronization. A monitor ensures that only one process can execute its procedures at any given time. Condition variables are used to block and wake up processes. Pr.Babahenini Med Chaouki (Biskra Univ.) ME October 2, 2025 25 / 25
title: Monitors
subject: se

text:
[OS]
Lecture for L3 – SE2 (2025/2026) Prof. Babahenini Mohamed Chaouki Mohamed Khider Biskra University Computer Science Department December 9, 2025 Pr. Babahenini Med Chaouki (Biskra Univ.) Deadlock December 9, 2025 1 / 25
title: OS
subject: se

text:
[Outline]
1 Introduction to Deadlocks 2 Characterization of Deadlocks 3 Deadlock Detection and Recovery 4 Deadlock Avoidance 5 Preventing Deadlocks Pr. Babahenini Med Chaouki (Biskra Univ.) Deadlock December 9, 2025 2 / 25 Introduction to Deadlocks
title: Outline
subject: se

text:
[Deadlock?]
Deadlock occurs when processes block each other indefinitely while waiting for resources. Examples of resources: CPU cycles, memory, files, printers. Common in multi-tasking and distributed systems. Example Scenario: Process 1 holds Resource 1 and requests Resource 2. Process 2 holds Resource 2 and requests Resource 1. Pr. Babahenini Med Chaouki (Biskra Univ.) Deadlock December 9, 2025 3 / 25 Introduction to Deadlocks
title: Deadlock?
subject: se

text:
[example]
Traffic only in one direction. Each section of a bridge can be viewed as a resource. If a deadlock occurs, it can be resolved if one car backs up (preempt resources and rollback). Several cars may have to be backed up if a deadlock occurs. Starvation is possible Pr. Babahenini Med Chaouki (Biskra Univ.) Deadlock December 9, 2025 4 / 25 Characterization of Deadlocks
title: example
subject: se

text:
[Deadlock]
Deadlock can arise if four conditions hold simultaneously. 1 Mutual Exclusion: only one process can use a resource at a time. Resources cannot be shared. 2 Hold and Wait: a process holding at least one resource is waiting to acquire additional resources held by other processes. 3 No Preemption: a resource can be released only voluntarily by the process holding it, after that process has completed its task. 4 Circular Wait: A set of processes form a cycle, each waiting for a resource held by the next. All four conditions must hold simultaneously for a deadlock to occur. Pr. Babahenini Med Chaouki (Biskra Univ.) Deadlock December 9, 2025 5 / 25 Characterization of Deadlocks
title: Deadlock
subject: se

text:
[(RAG)]
A directed graph representing processes and resources.(A set of vertices V and a set of edges E). Nodes: Circular nodes represent processes. Rectangular nodes represent resources. Edges: Request edge: process → resource. Allocation edge: resource → process. Pr. Babahenini Med Chaouki (Biskra Univ.) Deadlock December 9, 2025 6 / 25 Characterization of Deadlocks
title: (RAG)
subject: se

text:
[RAG]
Pr. Babahenini Med Chaouki (Biskra Univ.) Deadlock December 9, 2025 7 / 25 Characterization of Deadlocks
title: RAG
subject: se

text:
[Recovery]
Detection and Recovery: When deadlocks occur despite preventive measures, the operating system must identify and resolve them. Detection algorithms like the Wait-For Graph analyze system states to pinpoint deadlocks. Once detected, recovery methods like Process Rollback or Process Termination are employed. The recovery algorithm releases the resources held by one or more processes, allowing the system to progress. Prevention: The operating system proactively ensures that deadlocks cannot occur by always maintaining the system in a safe state. This involves carefully controlling resource allocation to processes. Techniques like the Banker’s Algorithm are commonly used, where the system evaluates requests to guarantee that granting them will not lead to an unsafe state. Pr. Babahenini Med Chaouki (Biskra Univ.) Deadlock December 9, 2025 8 / 25 Deadlock Detection and Recovery
title: Recovery
subject: se

text:
[Deadlocks]
Steps to Detect Deadlocks: 1 Construct a Resource Allocation Graph (RAG). 2 Look for cycles in the graph. 3 For multiple instances of resources, use a detection algorithm. Example Detection Algorithm: Work array tracks available resources. Finish array flags if processes can complete. Simulate resource allocation and check for blocked processes. Pr. Babahenini Med Chaouki (Biskra Univ.) Deadlock December 9, 2025 9 / 25 Deadlock Detection and Recovery
title: Deadlocks
subject: se

text:
[Methods]
Strategies for Recovery: 1 Terminate Processes: Abort all deadlocked processes. Abort one process at a time until the deadlock is resolved. 2 Preempt Resources: Select a process to preempt resources from. Rollback the process if necessary. Challenges: High overhead for detecting deadlocks. Risk of starvation for some processes. Pr. Babahenini Med Chaouki (Biskra Univ.) Deadlock December 9, 2025 10 / 25 Deadlock Avoidance
title: Methods
subject: se

text:
[States]
Safe State: If a system is in the safe state, there are no deadlocks. Unsafe State: If a system is in an unsafe state, there is a possibility of deadlock. Avoidance deadlock: Avoidance ensures that a system will never enter an unsafe state. Pr. Babahenini Med Chaouki (Biskra Univ.) Deadlock December 9, 2025 11 / 25 Deadlock Avoidance
title: States
subject: se

text:
[Algorithm]
Introduction the Banker’s Algorithm: 1 Multiple instances. 2 Each process must a priori claim maximum use. 3 When a process requests a resource, it may have to wait. 4 When a process gets all its resources it must return them in a finite amount of time Steps in the Banker’s Algorithm: 1 Check if the request is less than the current Need. 2 Check if resources are available to fulfill the request. 3 Temporarily allocate resources and check if the system remains in a safe state. 4 If the system is safe, grant the request; otherwise, deny it. Pr. Babahenini Med Chaouki (Biskra Univ.) Deadlock December 9, 2025 12 / 25 Deadlock Avoidance
title: Algorithm
subject: se

text:
[Algorithm]
1 Initialize: Define available resources, allocated resources, and maximum resources for each process. 2 Request Resources: Check if the request is valid: Request ≤ Need Request ≤ Available 3 Simulate Allocation: Temporarily allocate the resources. 4 Check Safe State: Ensure that the system remains in a safe state after allocation. 5 Approve or Deny: Approve if safe, deny otherwise. Pr. Babahenini Med Chaouki (Biskra Univ.) Deadlock December 9, 2025 13 / 25 Deadlock Avoidance
title: Algorithm
subject: se

text:
[Algorithm]
Initialization: Define the following matrices: Available : Vector of resources currently available. Maximum : Maximum demand of each process for each resource. Allocation : Resources currently allocated to each process. Need : Resources each process still requires, calculated as: Need [ i, j ] = Maximum [ i, j ] − Allocation [ i, j ] Resource Request: A process P i requests resources Request [ i ] . Check: Request[i] ≤ Need[i] Request[i] ≤ Available If the above conditions are not met, deny the request. Pr. Babahenini Med Chaouki (Biskra Univ.) Deadlock December 9, 2025 14 / 25 Deadlock Avoidance
title: Algorithm
subject: se

text:
[Algorithm]
Pretend Allocation: Temporarily allocate the resources to P i by updating: Available = Available − Request[i] Allocation[i] = Allocation[i] + Request[i] Need[i] = Need[i] − Request[i] Pr. Babahenini Med Chaouki (Biskra Univ.) Deadlock December 9, 2025 15 / 25 Deadlock Avoidance
title: Algorithm
subject: se

text:
[Algorithm]
Safety Check: Perform a safety algorithm to determine if the system is in a safe state: 1 Initialize Work = Available and Finish[i] = false for all i . 2 Find a process P i such that: Need[i] ≤ Work 3 If such a process is found: Update Work = Work + Allocation[i] and Mark Finish[i] = true. 4 Repeat until all processes are marked finished (Finish[i] = true for all i ). If all processes finish, the state is safe; otherwise, it’s unsafe. Pr. Babahenini Med Chaouki (Biskra Univ.) Deadlock December 9, 2025 16 / 25 Deadlock Avoidance
title: Algorithm
subject: se

text:
[Algorithm]
Decision: If the system is in a safe state, grant the request. If unsafe, rollback the allocation: Available = Available + Request[i] Allocation[i] = Allocation[i] − Request[i] Need[i] = Need[i] + Request[i] Pr. Babahenini Med Chaouki (Biskra Univ.) Deadlock December 9, 2025 17 / 25 Deadlock Avoidance
title: Algorithm
subject: se

text:
[Example]
5 processes P 0 through P 4 ; 3 resource types A (10 instances), B (5 instances), and C (7 instances). Snapshot at time T 0 Available: [3, 3, 2] Max:   7 5 3 3 2 2 9 0 2 2 2 2 4 3 3   Allocation:   0 1 0 2 0 0 3 0 2 2 1 1 0 0 2   Pr. Babahenini Med Chaouki (Biskra Univ.) Deadlock December 9, 2025 18 / 25 Deadlock Avoidance
title: Example
subject: se

text:
[Allocation)]
Definition: Need [ i, j ] = Max [ i, j ] − Allocation [ i, j ] Process A B C P 0 7 4 3 P 1 1 2 2 P 2 6 0 0 P 3 0 1 1 P 4 4 3 1 The system is in a safe state because the sequence: ⟨ P 1 , P 3 , P 4 , P 2 , P 0 ⟩ satisfies the safety condition. Pr. Babahenini Med Chaouki (Biskra Univ.) Deadlock December 9, 2025 19 / 25 Deadlock Avoidance
title: Allocation)
subject: se

text:
[Available]
To check if a request can be granted: Request ≤ Available Example: (1 , 0 , 2) ≤ (3 , 3 , 2) ⇒ true Thus the request is temporarily safe to consider . We now continue with a full safety check. Pr. Babahenini Med Chaouki (Biskra Univ.) Deadlock December 9, 2025 20 / 25 Deadlock Avoidance
title: Available
subject: se

text:
[Available]
Allocation Need Available Process A B C A B C A B C P 0 0 1 0 7 4 3 2 3 0 P 1 2 0 0 1 2 2 P 2 3 0 2 0 0 0 P 3 2 1 1 6 0 0 P 4 0 0 2 4 3 1 The safety algorithm yields: ⟨ P 1 , P 3 , P 4 , P 0 , P 2 ⟩ This is a safe sequence . Pr. Babahenini Med Chaouki (Biskra Univ.) Deadlock December 9, 2025 21 / 25 Deadlock Avoidance
title: Available
subject: se

text:
[(3,3,0)?]
Step 1: Check Request ≤ Need (3 , 3 , 0) ≤ (4 , 3 , 1) ⇒ true Step 2: Check Request ≤ Available (3 , 3 , 0) ≤ (2 , 3 , 0) ⇒ false Conclusion: Request (3,3,0) by P 4 cannot be granted. Pr. Babahenini Med Chaouki (Biskra Univ.) Deadlock December 9, 2025 22 / 25 Deadlock Avoidance
subject: se

text:
[(0,2,0)?]
Step 1: Check Request ≤ Need (0 , 2 , 0) ≤ (7 , 4 , 3) ⇒ true Step 2: Check Request ≤ Available (0 , 2 , 0) ≤ (2 , 3 , 0) ⇒ true Thus the request is possible . We now check via the safety algorithm: If granted, system still finds a safe sequence ⇒ safe to allocate Request (0,2,0) by P 0 can be granted. Pr. Babahenini Med Chaouki (Biskra Univ.) Deadlock December 9, 2025 23 / 25 Deadlock Avoidance
subject: se

text:
[Algorithm]
Advantages Prevents deadlocks by ensuring safe resource allocation. Allows systems to handle dynamic resource requests. Provides a systematic way to manage resources. Disadvantages Requires prior knowledge of maximum resource needs. High computational overhead for checking safe states. Impractical for systems with many processes or resources. Pr. Babahenini Med Chaouki (Biskra Univ.) Deadlock December 9, 2025 24 / 25 Preventing Deadlocks
title: Algorithm
subject: se

text:
[Techniques]
Eliminating Deadlock Conditions: 1 Mutual Exclusion: Use resources that can be shared. 2 Hold and Wait: Request all resources at once. 3 No Preemption: Allow resources to be preempted. 4 Circular Wait: Impose an ordering on resource requests. Trade-offs: May reduce resource utilization. Increases complexity of system design. Pr. Babahenini Med Chaouki (Biskra Univ.) Deadlock December 9, 2025 25 / 25
title: Techniques
subject: se

text:
[Monitors]
Lecture for L3 – SE2 (2025/2026) Prof.Babahenini Mohamed Chaouki Mohamed Khider Biskra University Computer Science Department November 11, 2025 Pr.Babahenini Med Chaouki (Biskra Univ.) Monitors November 11, 2025 1 / 19
title: Monitors
subject: se

text:
[Outline]
1 Introduction to Monitors 2 Structure of a Monitor 3 Condition Variables and Queues 4 The Hoare Monitor Model 5 The Hansen Monitor Model 6 Comparing Hoare and Hansen Models 7 Condition variables and semaphores 8 Examples Pr.Babahenini Med Chaouki (Biskra Univ.) Monitors November 11, 2025 2 / 19 Introduction to Monitors
title: Outline
subject: se

text:
[Tools]
Semaphores and Mutexes were among the first tools for managing concurrency. Challenges with semaphores: Complexity: Requires careful programming to avoid deadlock and race conditions. Error-Prone: Easy to mismanage ‘wait()‘ and ‘signal()‘ calls, leading to inconsistencies. Lack of Structure: Does not provide high-level abstractions; all synchronization is manual. Need for a more structured and reliable synchronization mechanism, leading to the development of monitors. Pr.Babahenini Med Chaouki (Biskra Univ.) Monitors November 11, 2025 3 / 19 Introduction to Monitors
title: Tools
subject: se

text:
[Monitors]
Monitors were introduced by C.A.R. Hoare and Per Brinch Hansen in the 1970s. Aimed to simplify synchronization by providing structured control over shared resources. Key innovation: Encapsulation of both data and the procedures to access it, allowing for safe access to shared resources. Monitors added condition variables to handle specific conditions within the monitor, reducing errors and enhancing readability. ”Monitors provide a way of encapsulating shared resources to en- force mutually exclusive access.” - Per Brinch Hansen Pr.Babahenini Med Chaouki (Biskra Univ.) Monitors November 11, 2025 4 / 19 Introduction to Monitors
title: Monitors
subject: se

text:
[Monitors]
Monitors are high-level synchronization constructs. Provide mutual exclusion and condition synchronization. Encapsulate shared resources and the code that accesses them. Suitable for handling complex concurrency patterns. Pr.Babahenini Med Chaouki (Biskra Univ.) Monitors November 11, 2025 5 / 19 Structure of a Monitor
title: Monitors
subject: se

text:
[Monitor]
A monitor consists of: Procedures: Functions defined within the monitor to access and modify shared resources. Condition Variables: Used for conditional synchronization; allow processes to wait or be notified. Internal Data: The shared resources managed by the monitor. Entry Queue: Holds processes waiting to enter the monitor. Condition Queues: Separate queues for each condition variable. Pr.Babahenini Med Chaouki (Biskra Univ.) Monitors November 11, 2025 6 / 19 Structure of a Monitor
title: Monitor
subject: se

text:
[structure]
Figure: Basic Structure of a Monitor with example Pr.Babahenini Med Chaouki (Biskra Univ.) Monitors November 11, 2025 7 / 19 Structure of a Monitor Figure: Basic Structure of a Monitor Pr.Babahenini Med Chaouki (Biskra Univ.) Monitors November 11, 2025 8 / 19 Structure of a Monitor
title: structure
subject: se

text:
[Work]
Only one process can execute within the monitor at a time (mutual exclusion). If a process needs to wait, it releases the monitor and waits on a condition variable. Condition variables handle synchronization by: wait(): Causes a process to release the monitor lock and wait on the condition. signal(): Wakes up one waiting process on that condition, if any. processes waiting on a condition variable are added to that variable’s queue. Pr.Babahenini Med Chaouki (Biskra Univ.) Monitors November 11, 2025 9 / 19 Condition Variables and Queues
title: Work
subject: se

text:
[Queues]
Condition Variables are used for signaling in monitors: Allow processes to wait for specific conditions to be met. Each condition variable has an associated queue. wait() operation: Suspends the calling process and places it in the condition’s queue. signal() operation: Wakes up one process from the condition queue. If no process is waiting, the signal has no effect. Pr.Babahenini Med Chaouki (Biskra Univ.) Monitors November 11, 2025 10 / 19 The Hoare Monitor Model
title: Queues
subject: se

text:
[Model]
Immediate Transfer of Control: When a process signals, the waiting process immediately gains control. Maintains stronger guarantees on condition variables. Useful for systems requiring high responsiveness. Example (Hoare Model Pseudocode) if (!condition) { condVar.wait(); } critical_section(); Pr.Babahenini Med Chaouki (Biskra Univ.) Monitors November 11, 2025 11 / 19 The Hoare Monitor Model
title: Model
subject: se

text:
[Variables]
Example (Pseudocode for Condition Variable Use) monitor BoundedBuffer { int buffer[N]; int count = 0; condition notFull, notEmpty; void insert(item) { if (count == N) notFull.wait(); buffer[count++] = item; notEmpty.signal(); } void remove() { if (count == 0) notEmpty.wait(); item = buffer[--count]; notFull.signal(); } } Pr.Babahenini Med Chaouki (Biskra Univ.) Monitors November 11, 2025 12 / 19 The Hoare Monitor Model The insert() function waits if the buffer is full and signals notEmpty after adding an item. The remove() function waits if the buffer is empty and signals notFull after removing an item. Pr.Babahenini Med Chaouki (Biskra Univ.) Monitors November 11, 2025 13 / 19 The Hansen Monitor Model
title: Variables
subject: se

text:
[Model]
Delayed Transfer of Control: When a process signals, it continues executing until it exits the monitor. Less immediate than the Hoare model but simpler for non-time-sensitive tasks. Example (Hansen Model Pseudocode) while (!condition) { condVar.wait(); } critical.section(); Pr.Babahenini Med Chaouki (Biskra Univ.) Monitors November 11, 2025 14 / 19 Comparing Hoare and Hansen Models
title: Model
subject: se

text:
[Models]
Hoare Model: Immediate execution for waiting for processes. Ideal for situations where responsiveness is critical. The condition is assumed to hold after a process is signaled and enters the critical section immediately. Hansen Model: Delayed transfer of control. Simpler implementation, suitable for general applications. The process must recheck the condition after waking up because other processes may have modified the state in the meantime. Pr.Babahenini Med Chaouki (Biskra Univ.) Monitors November 11, 2025 15 / 19 Condition variables and semaphores
title: Models
subject: se

text:
[semaphores]
Condition variables != semaphores A lock controls access to the monitor Wait: blocks the process and abandons the monitor lock. To call wait, the process must be in the monitor, hence the lock. The P() semaphore only blocks the thread if the value is less than 0. Signal: causes the waiting thread to wake up If there is no waiting process, the signal is lost V() increments the value, so future processes do not need to wait for P() Condition variables have no history! However, they can be used to implement each other. Pr.Babahenini Med Chaouki (Biskra Univ.) Monitors November 11, 2025 16 / 19 Condition variables and semaphores
title: semaphores
subject: se

text:
[monitors]
Advantages Simplifies synchronization logic compared to semaphores. Reduces common errors like deadlocks and race conditions. Promotes modular and encapsulated design. Disadvantages Limited expressiveness for complex synchronization scenarios. Monitor implementations may introduce hidden overhead. Not directly supported by all programming languages—requires additional libraries or frameworks in some cases. Pr.Babahenini Med Chaouki (Biskra Univ.) Monitors November 11, 2025 17 / 19 Examples
title: monitors
subject: se

text:
[Columns)]
Algorithm ReadersWriters Monitor (Part 1) 1: variable readCount = 0 2: variable writerPresent = false 3: condition okToRead, okToWrite 4: procedure startRead 5: if writerPresent then 6: wait(okToRead) 7: end if 8: readCount = readCount + 1 9: end procedure 10: procedure endRead 11: readCount = readCount - 1 12: if readCount == 0 then 13: signal(okToWrite) 14: end if 15: end procedure Algorithm ReadersWriters Monitor (Part 2) 1: procedure startWrite 2: if writerPresent or readCount ¿ 0 then 3: wait(okToWrite) 4: end if 5: writerPresent = true 6: end procedure 7: procedure endWrite 8: writerPresent = false 9: signal(okToWrite) 10: signal(okToRead) 11: end procedure Pr.Babahenini Med Chaouki (Biskra Univ.) Monitors November 11, 2025 18 / 19 Examples
title: Columns)
subject: se

text:
[Explanation]
Multiple readers can read concurrently (tracked by readCount ). Writers have exclusive access ( writerPresent controls this). Readers wait if a writer is active; writers wait if readers or other writers are active. okToRead and okToWrite are condition variables for synchronization. wait releases the monitor lock and blocks the thread until signaled. signal wakes waiting threads when conditions change. Pr.Babahenini Med Chaouki (Biskra Univ.) Monitors November 11, 2025 19 / 19
title: Explanation
subject: se

text: 06  October 2025 Mohamed Khider BISKRA UNIVERSITY Department of Computer Science Operating Systems II Academic Year 2025/2026 Exercise series 2: Synchronisation of processes Exercise 1: 1. Consider the following 3 processes: P1 Cycle Display("X") ; End Cycle P2 Cycle Display("Y") ; End Cycle P3 Cycle Display("Z") ; End Cycle Use semaphores to synchronise the P1, P2 and P3 processes to comply with the following constraints: 1. The character string displayed must be: XYZXYZXYZ... 2. The number of ‘X’ displayed must always be greater than or equal to the number of ‘Z’ displayed. 3. Process P2 must start only after process P1 has displayed ‘X’ and process P3 has completed its previous cycle. A precedence graph is a directed, acyclic graph. Nodes represent tasks, and arcs indicate the order in which tasks are to be accomplished. In particular, a task can execute as soon as all its predecessors have been completed.
subject: se

text: Assume that the tasks are processes and that each process has the following outline: process T {wait for predecessors, if any; body of the task; signal successors, if any;} Using semaphores, show how to synchronize five processes whose permissible execution is specified by the precedence graph in following figure. Minimize the number of semaphores that you use, and do not impose constraints not specified in the graph. Exercise 2 : Given the following routine : print() { process P1 { write(‘‘line 1’’); write(‘‘line 2’’);} process P2 { write(‘‘line 3’’); write(‘‘line 4’’); } process P3 { write(‘‘line 5’’); write(‘‘line 6’’); } } 1. How many different outputs could this program produce? Explain your reasoning. 2. Add semaphores to the program so that the six lines of output are printed in the order 1,2,3,4,5,6. Declare and initialize any semaphores you need and add P and V operations to the above processes. Exercise 3 : The model of readers and writers illustrates a situation encountered in the management of shareable files. In this model, we consider parallel processes (n at most) divided into two classes, readers and writers. These processes can share a single resource, the file.
subject: se

text: This file can be read simultaneously by several readers, while the writers must have exclusive access to it (only one writer can write to it and no reader can read it during this time). The readers‘ and writers’ schedules are as follows: READERS reading request ; reading ; end of reading ; WRITERS writing request ; writing ; end of writing ; The write request and end of writing procedures must ensure mutual exclusion between two writers. With the read request and end of reading procedures, they must ensure the rules of co-operation between the class of readers and the class of writers. Using semaphores, P and V operations, and state variables, write the readers‘and writers’ programs for the case where readers always have priority over writers without being requested; the only case where a reader has to wait is when a writer occupies the file. An editor can therefore only access the file if no reader is waiting or reading. Any coalition of readers is allowed to occupy the file indefinitely and prevent editors from accessing it. Exercise 4 : In a shared flat, a kitchen is shared between cooks preparing sweet dishes and those preparing savoury dishes.
subject: se

text: To avoid mixing flavours or smells, having cooks from these two categories in the kitchen is forbidden. Two classes of process are considered: Sweet Cook and Savoury Cook, described as follows: A) Cook_Sweet process : 1- Request for access to the kitchen to prepare a sweet dish; 2- Preparation of a sweet dish;3- Exit from the kitchen. B) Savoury cook process : 1- Request for access to the kitchen to prepare a savoury dish; 2- Preparation of a savoury dish; 3- Exit from the kitchen. 1) Propose a solution for synchronising access to the kitchen to ensure that the sweet and savoury cooks are never in the kitchen at the same time by : a. Defining the necessary semaphores and their initial values. b. Implementing the following functions: - acces_kitchen_sugar() and exit_kitchen_sugar() ; - acces_kitchen_ savoury () and exit_kitchen_ savoury (). Exercise 5 : The hairdressing salon has a hairdresser C, a chair F in which the customer sits to have his hair done and N seats to wait and N seats for waiting. - If there are no customers, hairdresser C dozes in chair F. - When a customer arrives and C is asleep, he wakes him up, and C gets up. The customer sits in F and has his hair done.
subject: se

text: - If a customer arrives while the hairdresser is working:  if one of the N seats is free, the customer sits down and waits,  if not, they leave. 1. Define the data needed to simulate the problem. 2. Write the process procedures for synchronising the hairdresser's and his customers' activities using semaphores. Exercise 6 : We want to implement a shared stack. The stack() and unstack() functions can be called simultaneously by several processes. The stack() function blocks until there is free space in the stack. The unstack() function blocks until data is available on the stack. Finally, the processes must exclude each other. Use the semaphore synchronisation mechanisms to implement this behaviour. Exercise 7: Let s be a semaphore, defined by the following variables: - i(s): Initial value of the semaphore s, with i(s)≥ 0. - np(s): Number of P operations performed on s. - nv(s): Number of V operations performed on s. - nf'(s): Number of processes currently waiting in the queue for the semaphore s.
subject: se

text: Verify that the following relationship holds: nf'(s) = max(0, np(s) - (i(s) + nv(s))) This relationship states that the number of processes waiting for the semaphore is the maximum of zero or the difference between the number of P operations and the sum of the initial value of the semaphore and the number of V operations performed. Exercise 8: The following table 1 shows the sequence of semaphore operations at the beginning and at the end of the tasks A, B, C. Table 1 : Sequence of semaphore operations Determine for the 4 cases a,b,c,d, given in the table 1.1, whether and in which sequence the tasks are executed, using the initializations of the semaphore variables given in table 2. Variable a b c d SA 2 3 2 0 SB 0 0 1 0 SC 2 2 1 3 Table 2: Initial values of the semaphore variables
subject: se

text: 09 November 2025 Mohamed Khider BISKRA UNIVERSITY Department of Computer Science Operating Systems II Academic Year 2025/2026 Exercise series 3: Synchronisation of processes using monitor Questions : 1. Explain the difference between Hoare’s and Hansen’s monitor synchronization models. 2. How does each model handle signaling when a process is waiting on a condition variable? 3. What are the potential implications of these differences for program performance and correctness? 4. Describe the role of condition variables in monitor synchronization. 5. In Hoare’s model, what happens to the process that signals a condition variable? 6. In Hansen’s model, how does signaling work differently compared to Hoare’s? 7. When would it be more advantageous to use Hoare’s model over Hansen’s, or vice versa? Exercise 1 : In a hotel, two large rooms, A and B, are separated by a narrow door that can only be passed through by one person at a time. A PersonA process represents a person in room A who wants to go through the door. Similarly, a person in room B who wants to enter room A is represented by a PersonB process. There can be any number of processes A and B (i.e., people).
subject: se

text: Only one person can pass through the door at a time. process PersonA .. arrives in front of the .. door <<synchro>> .. walks through the door .. <<synchro>> .. end PersonneA process PersonneB .. arrives in front of the .. door <<synchro>> .. walks through the door .. <<synchro>> .. end PersonB A) When people are in both waiting rooms, there must be a strict alternation between the passage of people from A and B. In other words, after a person from A has passed through, a person from B must pass through if there is any waiting; otherwise, you continue with the people from A. The same applies to B. Question: Write a monitor and complete the PersonA and PersonB processes to synchronize their activities. B) To speed up throughput, when people are waiting in both rooms, up to ten (10) people from the same room can go through in succession (one after the other) before it is the turn of the people in the other room to go through. Modify or write a monitor to ensure this synchronisation. Exercise 2 : Two processes, A and B, communicate using a buffer T, which can only contain one message at a time.
subject: se

text: This buffer is used alternately for communication in both directions (only one process uses this buffer at a time). Process A deposits a message in the buffer, then waits for B's response before depositing another message, and so on... When B receives a message from A, it deposits its response in the buffer, waits for another message from A, and so on... 1. Write a monitor to synchronize processes A and B. 2. Suppose a third process, C, wants to communicate with B using the single T buffer. Processes A and C behave in the same way. B can, therefore, receive a message from A or C, and the response must be retrieved by the process that sent the message. Synchronise processes A, B, and C using a monitor. Exercise 3 : In a shared flat, a kitchen is shared between cooks preparing sweet dishes and those preparing savoury dishes. To avoid mixing flavours or smells, it is forbidden to have cooks from these two categories in the kitchen at the same time. Two classes of processes are considered: Sweet Cook and Savoury Cook, described as follows: A) Sweet Cook process : 1. Requests access to the kitchen to prepare a sweet dish. 2. Prepares the sweet dish. 3. Exits from the kitchen.
subject: se

text: B) Savoury Cook process : 1. Requests access to the kitchen to prepare a savoury dish. 2. Prepares the savoury dish. 3. Exits from the kitchen. 1. Propose a solution for synchronizing access to the kitchen to ensure that sweet and savoury cooks are never in the kitchen at the same time, using a monitor-based approach. 2. Define the necessary shared variables and condition variables inside the monitor. 3. Implement the following monitor procedures: o access_kitchen_sweet() and exit_kitchen_sweet() o access_kitchen_savoury() and exit_kitchen_savoury() 4. Explain briefly how mutual exclusion and synchronization are ensured by the monitor. Exercise 4 : A museum allows visitors to enter through the east entrance and leave through the west exit. Arrivals and departures are signaled to the museum controller by the turnstiles at the entrance and exit. At opening time, the museum director signals the controller that the museum is open and then the controller permits both arrivals and departures. At closing time, the director signal that the museum is closed, at which point only departures are permitted by the controller.
subject: se

text: In this exercise there are four processes/monitor: EAST, WEST, CONTROL and DIRECTOR. 1. Identify which of these should be processus and which one a monitor. Justify your response. 2. Provide the implementation of the monitor in pseudo code, by Implementing the following functions: Function requestEntry, function requestExit.
subject: se

text: 07 December 2025 Mohamed Khider BISKRA UNIVERSITY Department of Computer Science Operating Systems II Academic Year 2025/2026 Exercise series 4: Deadlock Exercise 1: Consider a system consisting of five processes (P0, P1, P2, P3, and P4) and three types of resources (A, B, and C). At time Ti, the system is described by : E = (10, 5, 7) and 1) Calculate Available and Need at time Ti. 2) Is the state of the system at time Ti healthy? Justify. 3) Assume that process P1 issues request R1 = (1, 0, 2). Will the request be granted immediately, or will it be put on hold? Justify this. Exercise 2: Consider an operating system with 6 types of resource (R1, R2 ... R6), only one of each type, shared in mutual exclusion by 3 processes P1, P2 and P3.
subject: se

text: These processes execute the following codes: P1() { while(1) { get (R4); get (R5); get (R3); // Use R4, R5, R3 Release(R4); Release (R5); Release (R3); } } P2() { while(1) { get (R3); get (R2); get (R6); //Use R3, R2, R6 Release (R6); Release (R2); Release (R3); } } P3() { while(1) { get (R1); get (R2); get (R5); // Use R1, R2, R5 Release (R5); Release (R2); Release (R1); } } The ‘get’ function allocates a resource to the calling process if it is free. Otherwise, it blocks the calling process until the requested resource is obtained. An allocated resource is released by the owner process when it invokes the ‘release’ function. 1. Give an execution sequence for the instructions in P1, P2 and P3 that leads to an interlock. 2. Can deadlocks be prevented by changing the order of resource requests? Justify this. 3. We want to avoid deadlocks by using the banker's algorithm. Should the ‘get’ function be modified? If so, explain how. Assume that the system is in the state where P1 holds resources R4 and R5, and P3 holds resources R1 and R2. Process P2 requests resource R3. Will the system satisfy this request? Justify your answer.
subject: se

text: Exercise 3: Consider a system at time t0 : Allocation Max Available A   B   C   D A   B   C   D A   B   C   D T0 0    0    1   2 0    0    1   2 1    5    2    0 T1 1    0    0   0 1    7    5   0 T2 1    3    5   4 2    3    5   6 T3 0    6    3   2 0    6    5   2 T4 0    0    1   4 0    6    5   6 1) Using the banker's algorithm, give the contents of the matrix Need and the vector E. Is the system in a healthy state? 2) If task T1 issues the request (0,4,2,0), can it be honored immediately? Exercise 4: Consider the following state of a system with multiple resource classes (r0,r1,r2). We have: 1. Determine the content of the Need and Available matrices. 2. Show that this state is a safe state. 3. If a request from process P1 (1,1,1) arrives, should it be granted immediately? Justify your answer.
subject: se

text:
[Exercise]
1 Answer in detail the following questions and justify your answers : 1. What is a process in the context of operating systems ? What is the main objective of process management in an operating system ? 2. What is a thread and how does it differ from a process ? 3. What is the role of a process scheduler in an operating system ? 4. What is context switching in process management and why is it necessary ? 5. What is the difference between preemptive and non-preemptive scheduling algorithms ? 6. What is the “Process Control Block” (PCB) in operating systems ? 7. What is the role of the process manager in operating systems ? 8. What is a zombie process in process management ? 9. What is the role of process priority in a multitasking operating system ? 10. What is the role of the system call fork() in process creation on Unix-like systems ? 11. Explain the concept of interprocess communication in operating systems. 12. What is the role of the init process in Unix-like operating systems ?
title: Exercise
subject: se

text:
[Exercise]
2 We are looking for a solution to the mutual exclusion problem, which consists of defining a set of state variables shared among the contexts of different processes. Authorization to enter the critical section will be defined by tests on these variables, and the possible waiting will be programmed as busy waiting, i.e., by repeatedly checking these tests. We restrict our study to the case of two processes, denoted p1 and p2. 1. First, try to construct a solution using a single boolean variable c , equal to true if one of the processes p i is in its critical section, false otherwise. Verify that mutual exclusion cannot be programmed using only this variable. 2. Construct a solution using a single shared variable t , with t = i if and only if process p i is allowed to enter its critical section ( i = 1 , 2). Write the program for process p i , then verify that this solution does not satisfy one of the mutual exclusion conditions. 3.
title: Exercise
subject: se

text:
[Exercise]
To avoid the previous limitation, we now introduce one boolean variable per process ; let c[i] be the variable attached to p i , with the following meaning : c[i] = true if process p i is in or requesting to enter its critical section, and c[i] = false if process p i is outside its critical section. Process p i has the power to read and modify c[i] and to read only c[j] . Write the program for process p i and then verify that this solution does not satisfy one of the mutual exclusion conditions. 4. A correct solution can be obtained by completing solution 3) with the introduction of an additional variable t to resolve conflicts when entering the critical section. This variable is modified only at the end of the critical section. The principle of the solution is as follows : in case of conflict (i.e., if 1 c[1] = true and c[2] = true ), both processes enter a waiting sequence where t retains a constant value. The process p i such that i ̸ = t cancels its request by setting c[i] = false , thereby letting the other process enter the critical section ; p i then waits in a loop until t is set back to i before re-requesting entry by setting c[i] := true .
title: Exercise
subject: se

text:
[Exercise]
Write the program for process p i , then verify that this solution satisfies the mutual exclusion conditions.
title: Exercise
subject: se

text:
[Exercise]
3 Suppose you are developing a multiprocess program using the TAS instruction to manage access to a shared critical resource. Here is the simplified pseudo-code of two processes attempting to access this resource : variable shared_resource = 0 variable lock = false Process 1: while (true) { while (TAS(lock) != false) { // Busy waiting } // Critical section shared_resource = shared_resource + 1 lock = false } Process 2: while (true) { while (TAS(lock) != false) { // Busy waiting } // Critical section shared_resource = shared_resource - 1 lock = false } 1. Explain how the TAS instruction works in this context. 2. What are the potential risks of this approach for managing the critical section ? 3. Propose an improvement or an alternative to this method to make critical section management more efficient and secure.
title: Exercise
subject: se

text:
[Exercise]
4 Propose a solution to the mutual exclusion problem using semaphores, then verify that this solution complies with the four conditions of mutual exclusion (as stated in class). In a computer system, there are three files F1, F2, and F3, and three processes whose programs A, B, and C have the following structures :
title: Exercise
subject: se

text:
[C]
Action C1 (read F3) Action C2 Action C3 Action C4 (write F2) Action C5 Each file cannot be read and modified at the same time. 1. Identify, for each file, the critical sections of A, B, and C. 2. Deduce the sections that must be executed in mutual exclusion. 2
